gravity.definition: Gravity is a force of attraction between all masses in the universe.
gravity.example: It keeps planets in orbit around the sun and causes objects to fall to the ground on Earth.
gravity.real_life_application: It allows us to walk, jump, and keeps water and atmosphere on Earth.
gravity.fun_fact: Gravity is weaker on the Moon, so astronauts can jump higher.

photosynthesis.definition: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into food.
photosynthesis.example: Plants take in carbon dioxide and water, and produce glucose and oxygen.
photosynthesis.real_life_application: It provides oxygen for animals and humans and forms the base of the food chain.
photosynthesis.fun_fact: Without photosynthesis, life on Earth could not exist.

evaporation.definition: Evaporation is when a liquid turns into gas, usually from the surface.
evaporation.example: Water from lakes or puddles turning into water vapor.
evaporation.real_life_application: It is part of the water cycle, helping distribute freshwater on Earth.
evaporation.fun_fact: Evaporation cools surfaces, which is why sweating cools the body.

atoms.definition: Atoms are the basic units of matter, consisting of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
atoms.example: Everything around you, including your body, is made of atoms.
atoms.real_life_application: Understanding atoms allows us to create chemicals, medicines, and electronics.

cells.definition: Cells are the basic building blocks of all living organisms.
cells.example: Humans have trillions of cells, like skin cells and blood cells.
cells.real_life_application: Studying cells helps develop treatments for diseases and understand life.
cells.fun_fact: The human body has more bacterial cells than human cells!

electricity.definition: Electricity is the flow of electric charge through a conductor.
electricity.example: When you plug in a device, electricity powers it.
electricity.real_life_application: It powers homes, computers, transportation, and almost everything modern.
electricity.fun_fact: Lightning is a natural form of electricity.

magnetism.definition: Magnetism is a force caused by the motion of electric charges.
magnetism.example: Magnets attract iron and steel objects.
magnetism.real_life_application: Used in motors, generators, and data storage devices.
magnetism.fun_fact: Earth itself acts like a giant magnet with a magnetic field.

force.definition: Force is any interaction that changes the motion of an object.
force.example: Pushing a door or pulling a rope applies force.
force.real_life_application: Understanding force is key in engineering, sports, and physics.
force.fun_fact: Sir Isaac Newton formulated the laws of motion describing forces.

energy.definition: Energy is the ability to do work or cause change.
energy.example: Kicking a ball or boiling water involves energy.
energy.real_life_application: Electricity, transportation, heating, and machines all rely on energy.
energy.fun_fact: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed.

friction.definition: Friction is the resistance to motion when two surfaces touch.
friction.example: Brakes on a bicycle create friction to stop it.
friction.real_life_application: Friction helps us walk, drive vehicles, and hold objects.
friction.fun_fact: Ice has low friction, which is why you slip easily on it.

sound.definition: Sound is a vibration that travels through a medium like air, water, or solids.
sound.example: When you clap, the vibration creates sound waves you hear.
sound.real_life_application: Used in music, communication, sonar, and medical imaging (ultrasound).
sound.fun_fact: Sound cannot travel in a vacuum.

light.definition: Light is electromagnetic radiation that can be seen by the human eye.
light.example: Sunlight allows us to see objects during the day.
light.real_life_application: Used in vision, photography, communication (fiber optics), and solar energy.
light.fun_fact: Light travels faster than anything else in the universe.

matter.definition: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space.
matter.example: Rocks, water, air, and humans are all matter.
matter.real_life_application: Understanding matter is fundamental in chemistry and physics.
matter.fun_fact: Matter exists in solid, liquid, gas, and plasma states.

temperature.definition: Temperature measures how hot or cold something is.
temperature.example: Boiling water has a high temperature, ice has a low temperature.
temperature.real_life_application: Used in weather, cooking, medicine, and scientific experiments.
temperature.fun_fact: Absolute zero is the lowest possible temperature (-273.15°C).

pressure.definition: Pressure is the force applied over a surface area.
pressure.example: Air pressure pushes on your body constantly.
pressure.real_life_application: Important in weather forecasting, hydraulics, and aviation.
pressure.fun_fact: Mount Everest has lower air pressure than at sea level.

voltage.definition: Voltage is the electrical potential difference between two points.
voltage.example: Batteries provide voltage to power devices.
voltage.real_life_application: Used in electronics, power systems, and circuits.
voltage.fun_fact: High voltage can be dangerous if not handled properly.

current.definition: Current is the flow of electric charge.
current.example: Electric current powers your home appliances.
current.real_life_application: Critical in electronics, electrical engineering, and energy distribution.
current.fun_fact: There are two types: AC (alternating current) and DC (direct current).

density.definition: Density is the mass of an object per unit volume.
density.example: Oil floats on water because it is less dense.
density.real_life_application: Used in material science, buoyancy calculations, and engineering.
density.fun_fact: Ice is less dense than liquid water, which is why it floats.

buoyancy.definition: Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on an object immersed in it.
buoyancy.example: Ships float because the water pushes them up.
buoyancy.real_life_application: Important in shipbuilding, submarines, and fluid mechanics.
buoyancy.fun_fact: Archimedes discovered the principle of buoyancy while bathing!

ecosystem.definition: An ecosystem is a community of living organisms interacting with their environment.
ecosystem.example: A forest includes trees, animals, insects, soil, and climate.
ecosystem.real_life_application: Studying ecosystems helps in conservation and environmental protection.
ecosystem.fun_fact: Every part of an ecosystem is interconnected; removing one species can affect many others.
